region_of_origin: India (origin); widely consumed in Singapore and Western café contexts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chai tea entry; the latte variant (with steamed milk) is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific chai latte sub-code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chai latte as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard café serving of chai latte is approximately 350 g (a tall-size cup), providing around 151 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 5.6 g fat, and 15.2 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chai Latte — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95 — this is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Saturated fat (1.43 g/100g), trans fat (0.09 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.14 mg/100g) were present in the input record attributed to a secondary commercial platform at confidence 0.70; these values are plausible and consistent with the milk-based composition, and are retained as supporting data with lower confidence flagging. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chai latte exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: sweetened milk tea / tea with milk and sugar, which has a reported GI of approximately 50–60 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A GI of 55 falls within the low GI category (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (4.35 g/100g × 350 g = 15.225 g) / 100 = 8.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar and low dietary fibre; sodium is moderate at 35 mg/100g (122.5 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request chai latte prepared without added sugar or with a sugar-free sweetener, as the added sugar and absence of dietary fibre trigger the sweetened flavoured beverage concern; sodium content is moderate and not the primary concern, but the overall beverage pattern should be limited.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar is an explicit ingredient (sugar-sweetened beverage); estimated GI 55 (low boundary) with GL ~8.4 per 350 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid the standard sweetened chai latte as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage; requesting preparation without added sugar or substituting a non-nutritive sweetener would substantially reduce glycaemic impact, and the spices (cinnamon, ginger) may confer modest insulin-sensitising benefit in an unsweetened preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.43 g/100g (5.0 g per 350 g serving) and added sugar; trans fat 0.09 g/100g is low but present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of the standard sweetened chai latte due to its saturated fat content from whole milk and the presence of added sugar; an unsweetened version made with low-fat or plant-based milk would be a more heart-healthy alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar negates anti-inflammatory benefit of chai spices (cinnamon, ginger, cloves)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite containing anti-inflammatory spices, the added sugar in the standard chai latte negates the anti-inflammatory benefit; an unsweetened chai latte would be suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and may provide modest anti-inflammatory support via cinnamon and ginger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chai latte is a warm, spiced milk-based beverage made by combining brewed black tea with steamed or frothed milk and a blend of aromatic spices including cinnamon, ginger, cardamom, and cloves, typically sweetened with sugar. Originating from the Indian masala chai tradition, the café-style chai latte has been widely adopted in Western coffee-shop culture and is popular in Singapore as a breakfast or snack beverage. The drink has a creamy, mildly sweet, and warmly spiced flavour profile with a smooth, frothy texture. Per 100 g, it provides 43 kcal, 2.81 g protein, 1.61 g fat (of which 1.43 g is saturated), 4.35 g carbohydrate (4.06 g sugars), 0 g dietary fibre, 35 mg sodium, 105 mg calcium, and 152 mg potassium. A standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 151 kcal. Clinically, the standard sweetened preparation is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension without modification. The spice blend (cinnamon, ginger) may offer modest anti-inflammatory and insulin-sensitising properties in an unsweetened preparation. The beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, but contains dairy and is not vegan.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chai latte?
Is chai latte suitable for hypertension?
What spices are in chai latte?
Is chai latte high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) — an approved primary regional source — at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C retained from secondary input data at confidence 0.70 and flagged. GI estimated from closest analogous food in University of Sydney GI Database (sweetened milk tea, Atkinson et al. 2008). Overall record confidence is high for the HPB-sourced fields.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chai latte is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this beverage entry. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for chai latte and no approved secondary source provides complete coverage for this specific preparation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; chai latte as a liquid beverage with filtered tea and milk contains negligible dietary fibre. 5. trans_fat_g (0.09 g/100g), vitamin_c_mg (0.14 mg/100g), and saturated_fat_g (1.43 g/100g) were sourced from a secondary commercial platform in the input record at confidence 0.70; these values are plausible and consistent with the milk-based composition and are retained but flagged as lower-confidence. 6. gi_category corrected to 'low': a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (GI ≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI classification system. 7. food_subgroup updated from 'Coffee' (input) to 'Hot milk-based beverages' as chai latte is a tea-based spiced milk beverage, not a coffee product. 8. The input record listed two portions both with is_default: true and both at 350 g; the serving size of 350 g is retained as the single default serving. 9. nutrient_source title and organisation updated to explicitly name HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as an approved primary regional source per Project Veritas v2 source policy, removing any reference to non-approved platforms as primary evidence.
